coredata.dc:title: Construction of a novel fluorescent nanocarrier with double hollow shells for pH-controlled release of imidacloprid and its distribution and transport in bok choy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi
Zhu, Meiqing
Shi, Taozhong
Ma, Xin
Wu, Xiangwei
Li, Qing X.
Hua, Rimao
coredata.dc:description: Nanotechnology has been widely used in the field of pesticides. Integration of nano-pesticides and carbon dot fluorescence can fully utilize the potential for high admission of pesticides on leaves and convenience observation of its distribution and transport in the tissues. In the present study, a fluorescent mesoporous nanosilica with double hollow shells for loading imidacloprid (Im@FL-MSNs) was designed and synthesized. The physical and chemical properties of the imidacloprid nanocarriers were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FT-IR spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and N2 adsorption/desorption. When the mass ratio of FL MSNs to imidacloprid is 6:5, Im@FL-MSNs exhibits good fluorescence properties, high loading efficiency (∼30%), great slow-release performance as well as pH controllability. Besides, Im@FL-MSNs can improve the ability of imidacloprid to adhere on the leaf surface of bok choy (Initial contact angled is greater than 80°）. Importantly, Im@FL-MSNs did not reduce the biological activity of imidacloprid (LC50 (95% CI) = 1.43 mg/L). It was able to visually study the absorption and distribution of imidacloprid in bok choy plants, and provide theoretical and technical guidance for pesticide reduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanocarrier
Imidacloprid
Controlled-release
Distribution and transport

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Release efficiency of imidacloprid in vitro
Distribution of imidacloprid and Im@FL-MSNs in bok choy
Characterization of physical and chemical properties
Uptake of Im@FL-MSNs in bok choy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis pathway of fluorescent double hollow-shell mesoporous nanosilica.
(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of FL-MSNs and Im@FL-MSNs. (b) Pore size distribution from BJH adsorption of FL-MSNs and Im@FL-MSNs.
The fluorescence properties of FL-MSNs by different treatment methods (a: Na2CO3 as a etchant, b: K2CO3 as a etchant).
Contact angle images (a) and values (b) of the imidacloprid, FL-MSNs dispersion and Im@FL-MSNs on bok choy leaves. Data are the mean ± SE (bars) (n = 3).
Effect of material ratio on the loading of imidacloprid with FL-MSNs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Data are the mean ± standard deviation, n = 3.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Indoor virulence of different imidacloprid formulations against vegetable aphids (Myzus persicae).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data are the mean ± SE (n = 3); 95% CI, confidence intervals at 95% probability.

head.ce:title: Construction of a novel fluorescent nanocarrier with double hollow shells for pH-controlled release of imidacloprid and its distribution and transport in bok choy

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanotechnology has been widely used in the field of pesticides. Integration of nano-pesticides and carbon dot fluorescence can fully utilize the potential for high admission of pesticides on leaves and convenience observation of its distribution and transport in the tissues. In the present study, a fluorescent mesoporous nanosilica with double hollow shells for loading imidacloprid (Im@FL-MSNs) was designed and synthesized. The physical and chemical properties of the imidacloprid nanocarriers were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FT-IR spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and N2 adsorption/desorption. When the mass ratio of FL MSNs to imidacloprid is 6:5, Im@FL-MSNs exhibits good fluorescence properties, high loading efficiency (∼30%), great slow-release performance as well as pH controllability. Besides, Im@FL-MSNs can improve the ability of imidacloprid to adhere on the leaf surface of bok choy (Initial contact angled is greater than 80°）. Importantly, Im@FL-MSNs did not reduce the biological activity of imidacloprid (LC50 (95% CI) = 1.43 mg/L). It was able to visually study the absorption and distribution of imidacloprid in bok choy plants, and provide theoretical and technical guidance for pesticide reduction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides protect crops against pests to ensure agricultural production (Jiang et al., 2020; Mattos et al., 2019; Shan et al., 2019). Most of the applied pesticides, however, do not stay on the target crops, but ended in soil and water bodies (Du et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020b). Pesticide residues pose a threat to the environment, human health, and food safety (Huang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019; Tzankov et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2022a, 2022b). Therefore, it is a scientific and technological problem to be solved. It is essential to understand the uptake and translocation of pesticides in plants and improve pesticide utilization and reduce the use of pesticides on the basis of effective pest control.

Compared with inorganic nanomaterials such as calcium carbonate and hydroxyapatite nanoparticles, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNs) have the characteristics of large pore surface area, adjustable size, high loading capacity, low preparation cost and good biocompatibility (Cao et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2015; Yi et al., 2015). It has been widely used in biosensing, pesticide, medicine, catalysis and other fields. Since Beck (Beck et al., 1992) first synthesized MCM-41 ordered mesoporous silica with uniform channels and adjustable pore size, MSNs have been taken as carriers for adsorption, catalysis and enzyme immobilization, and can realize controlled drug delivery system for drug targeted release, which has become a research hotspot in many disciplines. Zaharudin used MSNs as drug carriers to study the loading efficiency and release characteristics of MSNs to drug molecules (Zaharudin et al., 2020). Ali prepared novel lactoferrin (Lf)-coupled mesoporous silica nanoparticles for the combined delivery of pemetrexed (PMT) and ellagic acid (EA) (Ali et al., 2020). Under the action of static electricity and hydrophobicity, Lf-MSNPs loaded with dual drugs can be released sequentially and quickly to achieve the effect of drug delivery. Based on the successful application in the field of medicine, researchers extended the field of MSNs loading pesticides to control the release of pesticides and prolong the action time on the target. Liang crosslinked polyethyleneimine (PEI) with isocyanate functionalized SiO2 via urea bond could effectively improve the thermal stability and photostability of pendimethalin (Liang et al., 2017). Compared with pendimethalin emulsifiable concentrate (EC), it has a longer effective period, higher herbicidal activity, lower genotoxicity and less environmental pollution (He et al., 2013; Song et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Therefore, there is a need to develop a mesoporous nanosilica-based slow-release technology to improve pesticide utilization and reduce environmental pollution.

Although many studies on nano silica controlled-release of pesticides are reported, there are little research on the absorption, distribution and transformation of MSNs in plants based on fluorescence technology (Rasheed et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2021a, 2018b). The use of fluorescent MSNs as carriers has a positive effect on agriculture, which allows to observe how MSNs are transported, accumulated and even metabolized in plants (Kumar et al., 2019; Macaulay et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2020a, 2021b). Imidacloprid is a nitromethylene insecticide, which acts on nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (Elabasy et al., 2019; Eng et al., 2021; Gogos et al., 2012). The main action of imidacloprid is to block the motor nervous system of the pest, resulting in a failure of signal transmission (Poliserpi et al., 2021). In actual production and application, imidacloprid is often used in overdose, which leads to excessive residues in crops and also causes serious pollution to the water and soil (Hu et al., 2021; Wibowo et al., 2014; Yan et al., 2020). Imidacloprid is widely present in the environment and has been detected in the human body, and its potential toxicity has attracted attention (Zhu et al., 2018a). It is, therefore, of great practical significance to study the absorption dynamics and transport of imidacloprid in crops using nano fluorescent labeling technology combined with fluorescence microscopy technology.

The present study was inspired by the surface modification of hydrophilic groups and their combination with the fluorescence of carbon dots. The hollow MSN modified by carbon quantum dots and hydrophilic groups was prepared by high-temperature calcination, and imidacloprid was loaded. The drug delivery system not only prolonged the pesticide’s validity period, but also had adjustable fluorescence excitation and emission spectra as well as good fluorescence stability, which could realize the visualization of the carrier in bok choy plants. The loading of imidacloprid on MSNs under different conditions, the release performance of drug-loaded particles, the insecticidal activity against aphids (Myzus persicae) and the spatial and temporal distribution of MSNs in different parts of bok choy were discussed. The methods can improve the utilization rate of imidacloprid, decrease the application dose, reduce the risk of use, and provide theoretical and technical guidance for pesticide reduction.
The specific steps for the preparation of fluorescent nanocarriers loaded with imidacloprid are described in Supplementary information.

The loadings of imidacloprid on fluorescent nanocarriers were determined using an classical method (Cao et al., 2018). The load capacity of fluorescent nanocarriers was obtained using the formula:
(1)
LC ( % ) = (
W
Pesticide
/
W
MSNs
) × 100 %

In the above equation, W
Pesticide represents the weight of the pesticide embedded in the nanoparticles. W
MSNs is the weight of the nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release efficiency of imidacloprid in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
The 30 mg of Im@FL-MSNs was uniformly dispersed in 2.0 mL of release solution (PBS: EtOH:Tween-80 = 80:19.5:0.5, v/v/v), where PBS was configured to different pH (4.0, 5.4, 7.4, 8.6). The above solution was then transferred to the dialysis bag (MWCO 2000 g/mol). The prepared dialysis bags were placed into beakers containing the release solution and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 100 rpm. Samples were taken at different times for testing to obtain the release curve. The collected samples were subjected to HPLC analysis. The cumulative release of imidacloprid was calculated according to the following equation (Ao et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2022a, 2022b):
(2)
E
r
( % ) =
V
e
∑
i = 0
n = 1
C
i
+
V
0
C
n
W
Pesticide
× 100 %
where E
r Er is the release ratio of imidacloprid at different time. V
e is the volume of the sample collected (V
e = 1.0 mL). C
n (mg/mL) is the concentration of imidacloprid in samples collected at different times. V
0 is the total volume of the released solution, and W
Pesticide (mg) is the total content of pesticide.

Imidacloprid is analyzed using a Waters Acquity Ultra Performance Liquid Chromatograph (Milford, MA, USA), which equipped with an XDB-C18 chromatography column (150 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 µm particle size), a photodiode array (PDA) detector and a tandem mass spectrometer. The injection volume was 10 μL. The gradient mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and 1% formic acid in water with a flow rate of 0.3 mL/min. One standard solution sample is required for every ten samples tested. And every 30 samples need to be tested for an added recovery sample, the recovery rate does not meet the standard need to retest the batch of samples to ensure the accuracy of the test results.

The toxicity of the insecticide was assessed using a leaf immersion bioassay performed in 12-well cell culture plates. Imidacloprid is mainly diluted by acetone because of its volatile nature. Add 2% agar evenly to the bottom of the plate to maintain humidity. Round caps were used to cut bok choy leaves of the same size (20 mm diameter). The cut leaves were dipped into the configured solutions of different concentrations of imidacloprid for 20 s, than dried and placed in the culture plates. Adult asexual aphids (Myzus persicae) were carefully brushed onto the discs. The holes were covered with aperture-sized paper on both sides to prevent spillage. Each bioassay was consisted of six concentrations, where each concentration was replicated three times, with each replicate treating at least 15 aphids (Myzus persicae). Control aphids (Myzus persicae) were treated with distilled water containing 0.05% (v/v) Triton X-100. aphids (Myzus persicae) were maintained at a temperature of 23 ± 1 ℃ at 16:8 h (L:D) and scored for their mortality 48 h after treatment. aphids (Myzus persicae) that did not move at all when the aphid probed its legs with a soft paintbrush were scored as dead. LC50 values, slopes and 95% confidence limits were determined based on standard probability analysis using SPSS 22.0 (International Business Machines Corporation, New York, USA).

Morphologically intact leaves of bok choy were collected and rinsed with deionized water, then dried and set aside. The configured solution was dropped onto the leaf surface (500 mg/L) using a micro syringe. The dynamic contact angle was continuously detected by Kruss DSA 100 droplet shape analyzer (Hamburg, Germany) for 10 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distribution of imidacloprid and Im@FL-MSNs in bok choy
ce:section.ce:para: The bok choy tissues (roots, stems and leaves) were first frozen in a − 20 °C refrigerator for 6 h. The tissues of the bok choy were cut into uniform slices using the frozen sectioning technique. The bok choy tissue slices were placed on a glass slide, and covered with a cover glass to fix the bok choy tissue. The distribution of Im@ FL-MSNs in the bok choy tissues was observed under a Nikon inverted Tisingle bondS microscope (Tokyo, Japan, Ex = 375 nm).

Fluorescent double-shell mesoporous nanosilica (FL-MSNs) with well-defined morphology was prepared by a combination of selective etching techniques and in situ carbon dot preparation methods ( Fig. 1). Briefly, TEOS as raw material, APTES as cross-linking agent and CTAB as structural guide designed and synthesized nanosilica with bilayer hollow shell structure. Next, the bilayer structure was selectively etched with a mild etchant, sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), to obtain bilayer mesoporous nanosilica (MSNs). After multiple washes to remove the template CTAB, the resulting silica nanoparticles were calcined to produce fluorescent bilayer mesoporous nanosilica (FL-MSNs) with carbon dot mosaic and fluorescent properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of physical and chemical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of S-SiO2, MSNs, FL-MSNs and Im@FL-MSNs were observed using TEM (Fig. S1). S-SiO2 exhibited good independent monodisperse spherical shape with smooth and flat surface. The morphology of MSNs and FL-MSNs before and after segment firing is regular, with clear internal pores, uniform distribution, no obvious adhesion on the surface, and relatively regular morphology. Interestingly, compared to the FL-MSNs without imidacloprid, the uneven pores on the surface of Im@FL-MSNs disappeared and became smoother, which clearly showed that the pores of mesoporous silica were plugged, proving that the pesticide was successfully loaded onto the fluorescent hollow-shell mesoporous silica.

Characterization of XPS was used to analyze the elemental changes in FL-MSN and FL-MSN@Im (Figs. S2 and S3). The elements C, O, and Si were detected on FL-MSN, with binding energies of about 103.4 eV and 532.6 eV for Si2p and O1s, respectively (Table S1). In the XPS spectrum of FL-MSN@Im, new binding energy peaks at 399.65 eV and 205.24 eV appeared, corresponding to N1s as well as Cl2p, respectively, confirming the loading of imidacloprid onto fluorescent mesoporous silica. The binding energy peaks of about 284.28 eV, 399.65 eV, and 532.6 eV increased with the addition of C, N, and O elements in imidacloprid, further indicating the loading of imidacloprid onto fluorescent mesoporous silica. Not only that, the content of C/N/O/Cl in Table S2 is about 27% of the total amount, which is consistent with the loading rate tested previously.

The specific parameters of zeta potential and pore size of fluorescent nanocarriers before and after imidacloprid loading are shown in Table S1. Both FL-MSN and FL-MSN@Im were negatively charged with − 18.9 mV and − 19.8 mV, respectively (Fig. S4), and the particle sizes were 356.1 nm and 263.6 nm, respectively. Through the statistical analysis of more than 100 nanoparticle SEM images, the average diameter of the nanoparticles is determined. The average diameter of FL-MSNs is about 96.63 nm, and the average diameter of Im@FL-MSNs is about 108.2 nm. The different particle size results are due to the negative charge on the surface of nanoparticles, which is influenced by the forces between interfacial functional groups in the dispersion and coalescence, etc., resulting in larger particle sizes.

Self-excellent pore size properties are the criteria as an outstanding mesoporous nanosilica. Therefore, BET pore size experiments were performed ( Fig. 2). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms are typical type IV isotherms, indicating that FL-MSNs is an ordered mesoporous material. And the specific surface area, pore volume and pore diameter of FL-MSNs were 950.9 m2/g−1, 2.26 cm3g−1 and 2.56 nm, respectively. When imidacloprid was loaded onto the nanocarriers, the specific surface area (569.09 m2/g−1), pore volume (2.1719 cm3g−1) and pore diameter (1.22 nm) are reduced to different degrees (Table S3). The decrease of specific surface area further proved that imidacloprid was successfully loaded onto the carrier.

FT-IR spectra were used to study the changes of functional groups on the surface of fluorescent nanocarriers before and after loading with imidacloprid (Fig. S5). For FL-MSNs, the band at 2930.61 cm−1 is the stretching and bending vibration of Si-OH, while the typical band at 1655.10 cm−1 indicates the formation of an amide bond (-CONH-) between FL-MSNs and APTES. The two Si-O-Si characteristic peaks on the FL-MSNs structure appear at 942.86 cm−1 and 577.59 cm−1. The characteristic peaks of the imidacloprid structure appear at 1563.26 cm−1, 1440.82 cm−1, 1297.97 cm−1 and 716.32 cm−1 in the Im@FL-MSNs spectrum, and these characteristic peaks do not exist in the FL-MSNs spectrum. This indicates that imidacloprid has successfully attached to mesoporous silica.

The excitation intensity of MSNs selectively etched with different etchants at different emission spectra is shown in Fig. 3. Under dark conditions, the aqueous solution of carrier particles showed blue-green fluorescence under UV lamp irradiation compared with the control aqueous solution (Fig. 3 inset), indicating that the carrier materials have good fluorescence properties. Also, the results of fluorescence spectra of aqueous solutions of MSNs carriers with different etchants (Fig. 3a and b) further show that FL-MSNs have broad-spectrum fluorescence properties. FL-MSNs nanoparticles exhibit strong fluorescence when Ex = 320–380 nm, and the emission spectra are between 410 and 480 nm. And relative to the above comparison of fluorescence properties of two different carbon source materials, we selected the MSNs with the optimal fluorescence properties (using Na2CO3 as etching agent), labeled as FL-MSNs. The above fluorescence performance results indicate that the use of FL-MSNs fluorescent materials for loading pesticides can contribute to the visualization of the transport of drug-laden particles in target crops.

The ratio of pesticide to loading material has a great influence on the loading capacity of carrier material, and a higher loading capacity can improve the duration of the pesticide-loaded particles. The effects of different material to pesticide ratios on the loading of imidacloprid were studied. With the increase of carrier material, the loading of FL-MSNs showed a positive correlation, and the maximum loading of 29.34 ± 2.93% was achieved at the mass ratio of material to imidacloprid of 6:5 ( Table 1). However, the drug loading showed a decreasing trend with the increase of the material-to-pesticide ratio. The trend of drug loading of the carrier material was influenced by its drug loading capacity. Interestingly, the drug loading capacity gradually increased with the increase of the carrier material when it was below the drug loading capacity, while the opposite was true when it was above the drug loading capacity. The Im@FL-MSNs prepared at a drug material ratio of 6:5 were finally chosen for the study.

Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) can be used to measure the mass change of a sample at different temperature conditions, and is applied to study the heat stability. The thermogravimetric loss curves of FL-MSN, imidacloprid prodrugs and FL-MSN@Im are shown in Fig. S6. The samples were heated from 20 ℃ to 900 ℃, and the mass fraction of imidacloprid prodrugs decreased sharply from about 350 °C and degraded completely at about 800 °C. In contrast, FL-MSNs exhibited thermal stability and maintained almost constant mass in the studied temperature range. After the loading of imidacloprid, the nano-loaded particles Im@FL-MSNs also showed weight loss from about 350 ℃ with a weight loss rate of about 30%. The above results proved that imidacloprid was successfully loaded onto FL-MSNs with a loading rate of about 30%.

Excellent slow and controlled release performance is the basis for the development of pesticide-loaded particles. Therefore, controlled release performance studies of Im@FL-MSNs at different pH were performed. The release fitting results showed that the Ritger-Peppas equation was more suitable for fitting the release kinetics of drug-laden particles compared to the Higuchi fitting (Table S4). The Ritger-Peppas equation analyzes the release behavior of pesticides by judging the n value, when n < 0.45, diffusion is dominant; 0.45 <n < 0.89, there are two behaviors of diffusion and dissolution, and n > 0.89, dissolution is dominant. The n values of Im@FL-MSNs in acidic and neutral solutions are between 0.45 and 0.89, indicating that there are two behaviors, diffusion and erosion. However, the n value of Im@FL-MSNs under alkaline conditions is only 0.3946, which proves that the release under weak alkaline conditions is dominated by diffusion. This is Im@FL-MSNs affected by its appearance state as well as dissolution performance, the active ingredient was slowly dissolved and released in the dialysis bag, while nanosizing could improve the solubility of imidacloprid in the release medium, the dissolution and transmembrane of imidacloprid in the drug-loaded particles might be synchronized, showing a slight dissolution phenomenon. The release profile indicates that Im@FL-MSNs not only has a slow-release but also has the ability of pH-controlled release (Fig. S7). The slow release of the pesticide can reduce its rate of diffusion to the environment, which in turn can increase its stability. The pH-controlled release is valuable for application in different acidic and alkaline areas during agricultural production. Therefore, the preparation of imidacloprid nanocarriers can reduce the applied dose by releasing it slowly into the environment, which is important to reduce the health risk to non-target organisms.

The toxicity of Im@FL-MSNs against aphids (Myzus persicae) was evaluated indoors by the leaf dip bioassay. In order to study the virulence of FL-MSNs against aphids (Myzus persicae), two different formulations (imidacloprid prodrug, 70% water dispersible granules) were selected for comparison with them. All three formulations of imidacloprid showed satisfactory control of aphids (Myzus persicae), but the insecticidal effect was partially different between formulations (Fig. S8). The LC50 of aphids (Myzus persicae) to imidacloprid, 70% water dispersible granules and Im@FL-MSNs were 1.67, 1.35 and 1.43 mg/L, respectively ( Table 2). The results showed that the inhibitory effect of Im@FL-MSNs on aphids (Myzus persicae) was not significantly different from or even slightly enhanced by commercial pesticide formulations. That is, Im@FL-MSNs does not affect the aphid control effect of imidacloprid itself, and has the potential of further development and application.

As a pesticide nanocarrier, its adhesion characteristics on the crop is one of the key factors to evaluate its ability to enhance pesticide deposition. On this basis, we investigated the contact angle changes of imidacloprid fluorescent nanocarriers on Bok choy at different times. As shown in the Fig. 4, while the contact angle of imidacloprid prodrug was slightly larger than that of FL-MSNs and Im@FL-MSNs in the initial stage, the contact angle reduction of FL-MSNs and Im@FL-MSNs was much smaller than that of imidacloprid prodrug within 10 min after spraying, reaching 34.7%, while FL-MSNs and Im@FL-MSNs are only 20.3% and 12.6%. This is attributed to the fact that FL-MSNs have improved affinity for crop leaves. In summarizing, FL-MSNs have the potential to improve the deposition and utilization efficiency of imidacloprid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake of Im@FL-MSNs in bok choy
ce:section.ce:para: The visualization of Im@FL-MSNs uptake and translocation in bok choy is shown in Fig. S9. Compared with the blank control, the fluorescence uptake and transport of Im@FL-MSNs could be observed by fluorescence microscopy in the treatment group, which proved that Im@FL-MSNs could be absorbed and conducted by the root. Not only that, the distribution and migration of Im@FL-MSNs in bok choy at different moments of time could also be observed visually (Table S5). That is, the technique can observe the conduction and transport of Im@FL-MSNs in real-time, which provides an important reference for the subsequent dynamic environmental behavior of pesticides in the crop.

In this work, fluorescent nano-SiO2 carriers loaded with imidacloprid (Im@FL-MSNs) were synthesized through simple modification of carbon dots and hydrophilic groups by ethyl orthosilicate as a precursor. The residual carbon source on the surface of Im@FL-MSNs was calcined to produce carbon dot material with good fluorescence properties. The inherent emission centers originating from the carbon dots can overcome the disadvantages of environmental toxicity associated and photobleaching. The double-layer hollow shell design somewhat enhances the carrying capacity of FL-MSNs, regulates and prolongs their release rate. Influenced by the appearance state of Im@FL-MSNs and the dissolution properties, the structure of Im@FL-MSNs is disrupted under acidic conditions so that the release of imidacloprid can be controlled by adjusting the pH. The toxicological experiments effectively illustrated the potential of Im@FL-MSNs for practical applications. Interestingly, Im@FL-MSNs with fluorescent properties allowed to observe the migration and distribution in bok choy plants in real-time. This study provides a good research reference for visualizing the distribution and even metabolism of nanopesticides in plants.

Yi Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Meiqing Zhu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Taozhong Shi: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Data curation. Xin Ma: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Data curation. Xiangwei Wu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Data curation. Qing X. Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Rimao Hua: Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFD0200205), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32072464 and 42207489), the Academic and Technical Leaders Scientific Research Project of Anhui Province (No. 2020D253), the Scientific Research Start-up Fund for Introduced Talents of Anhui Polytechnic University (No. 2021YQQ052) and the USDA Hatch Project (HAW5032-R).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2022.114132.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
